target.name: phosphorylated zwitterionic hexasaccharide repeating unit of PS B from Bacteroides fragilis
target.motivation: Zwitterionic polysaccharides (ZPSs) from Bacteroides fragilis are attractive synthetic targets due to their unique immunological properties, including direct T-cell binding without protein conjugation, making them potential antigens for carbohydrate-based vaccines. PS B, a novel phosphorylated O-polysaccharide containing rare deoxy amino sugars and a 2-aminoethyl phosphonate moiety, has not been synthesized before. The synthesis aims to provide a well-defined molecule for structural and immunological studies.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Assembly of protected tetrasaccharide of PS A1 in low yield
First total synthesis of PS A1, identified correct glycosylation order
Total synthesis of PS A1 tetrasaccharide RU to study immune stimulatory properties
Total synthesis of PS A1 repeating unit
Synthesis of a trimer repeat unit of PS A1 oligosaccharide for detailed structural studies

success_criteria.free_text: First total synthesis of the structurally complex phosphorylated branched hexasaccharide RU of PS B via an efficient and stereoselective one-pot assembly, with successful installation of three 1,2-cis glycosidic linkages, glycosylation of D-quinovosamine donor with a sterically crowded, poorly reactive 4-OH galactose moiety, and late stage phosphorylation.

key_disconnections.bond_description: O4 reductive benzylidene ring opening of hexasaccharide 3
key_disconnections.reasoning: To access the 4-OH for late-stage phosphorylation.
To efficiently synthesize the complex hexasaccharide in a one-pot manner, requiring energetically well-differentiated glycosyl donors and acceptors for selective activation and coupling.

key_disconnections.bond_description: Disaccharide 5 from D-galacturonate donor 8 and 3-OH D-glucosamine acceptor 9
Disaccharide 6 from D-galactose donor 10 and 4-OH L-quinovosamine acceptor 11
key_disconnections.reasoning: Fragment 6 is a disaccharide diol acceptor.

key_disconnections.bond_description: L-quinovosamine acceptor 11 from L-quinovosamine donor 12 and PMPOH
key_disconnections.reasoning: To install the 1,2-cis linkage with alpha-selectivity.

key_disconnections.bond_description: Quinovosamine derivatives 12 and 7 from rhamnosyl triols 13 and 14
key_disconnections.reasoning: To access rare deoxy amino sugars via C2 inversion of triflates.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: A convergent one-pot synthetic strategy with protecting group normalization was designed. Benzyl (Bn), 2-naphthylmethyl (NAP), and benzyl carbamate (Cbz) were chosen as permanent protecting groups for mild hydrogenolysis. Paramethoxyphenyl (PMP) at the reducing end and NAP at the non-reducing end were strategically placed for orthogonal deprotection and chain elongation. The trichloroacetamido (TCA) group was used to mask amino groups and enable stereoselective beta-glycosidic bond formation. The strategy involved a (1+2+2+1) one-pot glycosylation, requiring careful selection of protecting groups to ensure orthogonal reactivity of donors and acceptors. Late-stage phosphorylation was planned after regioselective benzylidene ring opening.

thought.background: L-rhamnose triol 13 is an easily accessible starting material.
thought.gap: Need to selectively protect the hydroxyl groups of 13 to prepare for C2 inversion and subsequent glycosylation.
thought.decision: To selectively protect 13, converted it into a stannylene ketal, then reacted with benzyl bromide (BnBr) and tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB) to afford 3-OBn derivative 15 as a single regioisomer, expecting selective benzylation at C3.

outcome.observation: Compound 15 was obtained as an amorphous white solid.

evidence.text_span: The easily accessible L- rhamnose triol 13 was first converted into the corresponding stannylene ketal and further treated with benzyl bromide (BnBr) and tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB) at 85 °C to afford 3-OBn derivative 15 as a single regioisomer in 82% yield over two steps.

thought.background: Compound 15 has a free 4-OH group.
thought.gap: Need to selectively silylate the 4-OH group to differentiate it from the 2-OH for subsequent C2 inversion.
thought.decision: To selectively silylate the 4-OH of 2,4-diol 15, used tert-butyldimethylsilyl chloride (TBSCl) and imidazole, expecting selective silylation at the less hindered primary alcohol.

outcome.observation: Compound 16 was furnished as a white foam.

evidence.text_span: Selective silylation of 4-OH of 2,4-diol 15 using 1.5 equiv of tertbutyldimethylsilyl chloride (TBSCl) and imidazole at 85 °C furnished 2-OH rhamnose derivative 16 in 84% yield.

thought.background: Compound 16 has a free 2-OH group.
thought.gap: Need to invert the stereochemistry at C2 to obtain the L-quinovosamine configuration.
thought.decision: To achieve C2 inversion, subjected the C-2 OH to triflation using triflic anhydride (Tf2O) and pyridine, followed by SN2 displacement with sodium azide (NaN3) to give L-quinovosamine donor 12, expecting stereochemical inversion.

outcome.observation: L-quinovosamine donor 12 was obtained as a pale yellowish viscous liquid.

evidence.text_span: The C-2 OH was subjected to triflation using triflic anhydride (Tf2O), pyridine in CH2Cl2 and the so formed C2-Otriflate was subsequently displaced by sodium azide (NaN3) to give L-quinovosamine donor 12 in 86% yield over two steps.

thought.background: Compound 18 is a known precursor.
thought.gap: Need to protect the 3,4-diol of 18 and then benzylate the remaining free hydroxyl group to prepare for glycosylation.
thought.decision: To protect 18, reacted it with 2,2-dimethoxypropane (2,2-DMP) and p-toluenesulphonic acid (p-TSA) to form a 3,4-acetonide, followed by benzylation using NaH and BnBr to give the fully protected thioglycoside donor 19, expecting selective protection and benzylation.

outcome.observation: Compound 19 was obtained as a white foam.

evidence.text_span: Reaction of 18 with 2,2-dimethoxypropane (2,2-DMP) in the presence of p-toluenesulphonic acid (p-TSA) at RT for 2h followed by refluxing with MeOH:H2O (10:1) at 70 °C for 4 h afforded 3,4-acetonide protected compound (74%), which was then subjected to benzylation using NaH and BnBr to give the fully protected thioglycoside donor 19 in 92% yield.

thought.background: Compound 20 is an easily accessible 2,3-diol.
thought.gap: Need to selectively protect the hydroxyl groups of 20 and then perform a reductive ring opening to obtain the 6-OH derivative.
thought.decision: To selectively protect 20, converted it to a stannylene ketal, then reacted with BnBr and TBAB to furnish the 3-OBn derivative, followed by borane-mediated reductive ring opening at O6 to give 6-OH derivative 21, expecting selective protection and deprotection.

outcome.observation: The desired product 21 was obtained as a white solid.

evidence.text_span: For the synthesis of the galactose donor 22, the easily accessible 2,3-diol 20 was converted to the corresponding stannylene ketal by treating it with Bu2SnO in toluene at 110 °C and further reacting with BnBr and TBAB at 85 °C to furnish the corresponding 3-OBn derivative (74% yield over two steps), which upon borane mediated reductive ring opening at O6 gave 6-OH derivative 21 in 85% yield.

thought.background: Compound 21 is a 6-OH derivative.
thought.gap: Need to protect the remaining free hydroxyl groups to obtain a fully functionalized thioglycoside donor.
thought.decision: To fully functionalize 21, treated it with benzoyl chloride (BzCl) and pyridine in CH2Cl2 to provide fully functionalized thioglycoside donor 22, expecting benzoylation of the free hydroxyls.

outcome.observation: The desired product 22 was obtained as a white solid.

evidence.text_span: Treatment of compound 21 with benzoyl chloride (BzCl) and pyridine in CH2Cl2 provided fully functionalized thioglycoside donor 22 (85%).

thought.background: Compound 21 is a galactose diol.
thought.gap: Need to selectively oxidize the primary hydroxyl group to a carboxylic acid and then esterify it, followed by capping the C2-OH with a chloroacetyl group to obtain the D-galacturonate donor 8.
thought.decision: To obtain 8, performed selective TEMPO oxidation of 2,6-diol 21, followed by esterification using BnBr and NaHCO3, and subsequent capping of C2-OH with a chloroacetyl group, expecting selective oxidation, esterification, and protection.

reaction.temperature: 0 °C to rt then rt then 0 °C to rt

outcome.observation: Galactose ester derivative was obtained as a colourless viscous liquid, and then 8 as a white foam.

evidence.text_span: In parallel, the D-galacturonate donor 8 was obtained upon selective TEMPO oxidation of 2,6-diol 21 followed by esterification using BnBr and NaHCO3 and subsequent capping of C2-OH with a chloroacetyl group in a very good overall yield.

thought.background: Known D-rhamnnose triol 14 is the starting material.
thought.gap: Need to selectively protect the hydroxyl groups of 14 to prepare for C2 inversion.
thought.decision: To selectively protect 14, treated it with 2,2-DMP and camphorsulfonic acid (CSA) to afford the 2,3-isopropylidene derivative, followed by benzylation of the remaining free 4-OH using NaH and BnBr, and then deprotection of the isopropylidene group with p-TSA in MeOH:H2O to obtain 2,3-diol 23, expecting selective protection and deprotection.

outcome.observation: Diol compound 23 was obtained as a viscous liquid.

evidence.text_span: Accordingly, triol 14 was treated with 2,2-DMP in the presence of a catalytic amount of CSA to afford the corresponding 2,3-isopropylidene derivative and the remaining free 4-OH was benzylated using NaH and BnBr in DMF at 0 °C to give a fully protected derivative of D-rhamnose36. Subsequently, rhamnose derivative was again treated with p-TSA in MeOH: H2O (1:1) at room temperature to obtain 2,3-diol 23 in 78% yield, over three steps.

thought.background: Compound 23 is a D-rhamnose diol.
thought.gap: Need to selectively protect the hydroxyl groups of 23 to prepare for C2 inversion.
thought.decision: To selectively protect 23, converted it into a stannylene ketal, then reacted with NAPBr and TBAB to give 3-ONAP derivative 24, expecting selective naphthylmethylation at C3.

outcome.observation: Compound 24 was obtained as a white solid.

evidence.text_span: Stannylene mediated 2-naphthylmethylation on C-3 OH of compound 23 gave 3-ONAP derivative of D-rhamnnoside 24.

thought.background: Compound 24 has a free hydroxyl group.
thought.gap: Need to invert the stereochemistry at C2 to obtain the D-quinovosamine configuration.
thought.decision: To achieve C2 inversion, treated the remaining free hydroxyl group with Tf2O and pyridine, and the so formed triflate group was displaced by azide using NaN3 in DMF, to smoothly furnish D-quinovosamine derivative 25, expecting stereochemical inversion.

outcome.observation: Compound 25 was obtained as a white solid.

evidence.text_span: The remaining free hydroxyl group was treated with Tf2O and pyridine in CH2Cl2 at 0 °C and the so formed triflate group was displaced by azide using NaN3 in DMF, to smoothly furnish D-quinovosamine derivative 25, in 85% yield over two steps.

thought.background: Compound 25 is a D-quinovosamine derivative with an azido group.
thought.gap: Need to convert the azido group to an amine and then cap it with a TCA group to enable beta-selectivity in glycosylation.
thought.decision: To obtain 7, converted the azido group of 25 to amine using Zn dust and acetic acid, which was subsequently capped with TCA group using trichloroacetyl chloride in THF, expecting beta-selectivity in subsequent glycosylation due to neighboring group participation.

outcome.observation: Compound 7 was obtained as a white solid.

evidence.text_span: For obtaining requisite beta-selectivity in the ensuing (5+1) glycosylation via neighbouring group participation, azido group of compound 25 was converted to amine, which was subsequently capped with TCA group using trichloacetyl chloride in THF to afford orthogonally protected D-quinovosamine donor 7.

thought.background: Thioglycoside 12 is a stable and flexible donor.
thought.gap: Need to convert thioglycoside 12 into a more reactive Schmidt donor for glycosylation.
thought.decision: To convert 12 into Schmidt donor 12a, hydrolyzed thioglycoside 12 using N-bromosuccinamide (NBS) in THF:H2O, and then treated the resulting hemiacetal with trichloroacetonitrile and K2CO3, expecting a more reactive imidate donor.

outcome.observation: Desired hemiacetal was obtained as a viscous liquid, and then Schmidt donor 12a as a viscous liquid (quantitative yield).

evidence.text_span: Thioglycoside 12 being a stable and flexible donor could be readily converted into the corresponding Schmidt donor 12a (entries 3 and 4).

thought.background: Glycosylation of thioglycoside 12 with PMPOH is a key step to form the L-quinovosamine acceptor 11.
thought.gap: Achieving alpha-selectivity in L-quinovosamine glycosylation is challenging.
thought.decision: Attempted glycosylation of thioglycoside 12 under NIS/TMSOTf activation conditions in CH2Cl2/Et2O (1:3) with acceptor PMPOH at -30 °C, expecting good yield and moderate selectivity.

outcome.observation: The desired product 26 was obtained in good yield but with moderate selectivity (alpha:beta = 4:1).

evidence.text_span: Glycosylation of thioglycoside 12 under NIS/TMSOTf activation conditions in a dichloromethane/diethyl ether solvent mixture (1:3) with acceptor PMPOH at -30 °C afforded the desired product 26 in good yield but with moderate selectivity (alpha:beta = 4:1) (Table 1, entry 1).

thought.background: The previous attempt yielded moderate selectivity.
thought.gap: Need to improve the alpha-selectivity of the glycosylation.
thought.decision: Attempted glycosylation under identical conditions but lowered the temperature to -60 °C, expecting improved selectivity.

outcome.observation: The yield was greatly improved (98%), but there was no significant enhancement in selectivity (alpha:beta = 5:1).

evidence.text_span: By lowering the temperature to -60 °C (entry 2), we carried out glycosylation under identical conditions; in this case, though the yield of the reaction was greatly improved (98%), there was no significant enhancement in selectivity.

thought.background: Previous attempts with thioglycoside 12 showed moderate selectivity.
thought.gap: Need to improve the alpha-selectivity of the glycosylation.
thought.decision: Attempted glycosylation using imidate donor 12a with PMPOH and TMSOTf activation conditions in CH2Cl2/Et2O (1:3) at -60 °C, expecting improved selectivity due to the more reactive imidate donor.

outcome.observation: A moderate improvement in selectivity (alpha:beta = 10:1) was achieved.

evidence.text_span: A remarkable improvement in selectivity (alpha:beta = 20:1) and yield 91% over two steps) was achieved by performing glycosylation reaction using imidate donor 12a with PMPOH, TMSOTf activation conditions in a dichloromethane/diethyl ether solvent mixture (1:3) at -78 °C (Table 1, entry 4).

thought.background: Previous attempts with imidate donor 12a showed improved but not optimal selectivity.
thought.gap: Need to further optimize conditions to achieve higher alpha-selectivity and yield.
thought.decision: Performed glycosylation reaction using imidate donor 12a with PMPOH, TMSOTf activation conditions in CH2Cl2/Et2O (1:3) at a lower temperature of -78 °C, expecting a significant improvement in selectivity and yield.

outcome.observation: A remarkable improvement in selectivity (alpha:beta = 20:1) and yield (91%) was achieved. The newly formed linkage was confirmed as 1,2-cis (alpha) by 1H NMR and 13C NMR.

evidence.text_span: A remarkable improvement in selectivity (alpha:beta = 20:1) and yield 91% over two steps) was achieved by performing glycosylation reaction using imidate donor 12a with PMPOH, TMSOTf activation conditions in a dichloromethane/diethyl ether solvent mixture (1:3) at -78 °C (Table 1, entry 4). A doublet at 5.48 ppm with coupling constant J = 3.2 Hz for H-1 and 13C peak at 98.1 ppm for C-1 clearly confirmed that the newly formed linkage is 1,2-cis (alpha)

thought.background: Compound 26 is the alpha-linked L-quinovosamine derivative.
thought.gap: Need to deprotect the TBS group, reduce the azido group to an amine, and then cap the amine with a TCA group to obtain the L-quinovosamine acceptor 11.
thought.decision: To obtain 11, cleaved the TBS group in 26 using tetrabutylammonium fluoride (TBAF), reduced the azido group to amine using zinc in acetic acid, and capped the amine with TCA group using TCACl and THF, expecting a clean conversion to the acceptor.

outcome.observation: L-quinovosamine acceptor 11 was cleanly afforded as a sticky liquid.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, TBS group in 26 was cleaved in the presence of tetrabutylammonium fluoride (TBAF) in THF and the azido group was reduced to amine using zinc in acetic acid. The so formed amine was capped with TCA group using TCACl and THF to cleanly afford L-quinovosamine acceptor 11, in 78% yield over three steps.

thought.background: Donor 10 and acceptor 11 are available.
thought.gap: Need to perform glycosylation to form the disaccharide 27.
thought.decision: To form 27, activated beta-thiogalactoside donor 10 by NIS and TMSOTf in CH2Cl2:Et2O (1:3) and glycosylated with L-quinovosamine acceptor 11 at -60 °C, expecting a clean alpha-linked disaccharide as a sole isomer.

outcome.observation: The desired alpha-linked disaccharide 27 was cleanly afforded as a sole isomer in 82% yield. 1H NMR confirmed the alpha-linkage.

evidence.text_span: The beta-thiogalactoside donor 10 was activated by NIS and TMSOTf promoter in CH2Cl2:Et2O (1:3) as the participating solvent and glycosylated with L-quinovosamine acceptor 11 at -60 °C to cleanly afford the desired alpha-linked disaccharide 27, as a sole isomer in 82% yield {1 H NMR, delta 5.34 (d, J = 3.5 Hz, 1H, H-1), 5.24 (d, J = 3.0 Hz, 1H, H-1')}

thought.background: Compound 27 is a disaccharide with an isopropylidine acetal.
thought.gap: Need to remove the isopropylidine acetal to obtain the disaccharide diol acceptor 6.
thought.decision: To obtain 6, performed acidic hydrolysis of isopropylidine acetal in 27 in the presence of p-TSA, expecting clean deprotection.

outcome.observation: Desired disaccharide diol 6 was furnished.

evidence.text_span: Acidic hydrolysis of isopropylidine acetal in 27 in the presence of p-TSA furnished desired disaccharide diol 6 in 81% yield.

thought.background: For the synthesis of disaccharide acceptor 5, a coupling of D-galacturonate donor 8 with C3-OH D-glucosamine acceptor 9 was planned.
thought.gap: The D-galacturonate donors are known to be significantly less reactive.
thought.decision: Attempted glycosylation of trichloroacetimidate donor 8a with acceptor 9 using TMSOTf or TfOH as a promoter at -30 °C and 0 °C, expecting to form disaccharide 28.

outcome.observation: Donor got decomposed with time and acceptor was recovered as such. No desired product was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Initially, we tried glycosylation of trichloroacetimidate donor 8a with acceptor 9 using TMSOTf or TfOH as a promoter at -30 °C as well as 0 °C. Unfortunately, we were not able to get our desired product. In each case, donor got decomposed with time and acceptor was recovered as such (Table 2, entries 1 and 2).

thought.background: Previous attempts with trichloroacetimidate donor 8a failed.
thought.gap: Need to find a more stable donor for the glycosylation.
thought.decision: Attempted glycosylation using a more stable N-phenyltrifluoroacetimidate donor 8a' under identical conditions, expecting to form disaccharide 28.

outcome.observation: Led to the same result; acceptor was recovered.

evidence.text_span: Changing from imidate donor 8a to a more stable N-phenyltrifluoroacetimidate donor 8a', under identical condition led to same result (Table 2, entry 2).

thought.background: Previous attempts with imidate donors failed to form disaccharide 28.
thought.gap: Need to explore alternative activation conditions for the glycosylation.
thought.decision: Attempted coupling of thioglycoside 8 with acceptor 9 under activation of Ph2SO, Tf2O, TTBP at -60 °C, expecting to form disaccharide 28.

outcome.observation: Did not give the desired product. Orthoester 29 (34%) was obtained, and the remaining unreacted acceptor was recovered. Attempted rearrangement of orthoester 29 using TfOH did not lead to desired disaccharide 28.

evidence.text_span: Alternatively, the coupling of thioglycoside 8 with acceptor 9 under the activation of Ph2SO, Tf2O, TTBP at -60 °C did not give the desired product as well. In this reaction, orthoester 29 (34%) was obtained (Table 2, entry 3) and the remaining unreacted acceptor was recovered. Attempted rearrangement of orthoester 29 using TfOH did not lead to desired disaccharide 28.

thought.background: Direct glycosylation attempts to form disaccharide 28 failed due to low reactivity of the galacturonate donor.
thought.gap: Need an alternative strategy to synthesize the disaccharide acceptor 5.
thought.decision: Changed the strategy to a post-glycosylation oxidation and esterification. This involved synthesizing a disaccharide first, then oxidizing and esterifying it to obtain the desired galacturonate functionality.

outcome.observation: The strategy was changed to a post-glycosylation oxidation and esterification.

evidence.text_span: The electron withdrawing chloroacetyl group at C2 position and the ester group of galacturonate donor may account for the low reactivity in glycosylation. Therefore, we changed the strategy to a post-glycosylation oxidation and esterification as shown in Scheme 4.

thought.background: Thiogalactoside 22 is available.
thought.gap: Need to convert thiogalactoside 22 into a trichloroacetamidate donor for glycosylation.
thought.decision: To convert 22 into trichloroacetamidate 30, hydrolyzed thiogalactoside using NBS, and then treated the so formed hemiacetal with trichloroacetonitrile and DBU, expecting a reactive imidate donor.

outcome.observation: Desired hemiacetal was obtained, and then imidate donor 30 as a viscous liquid (quantitative yield).

evidence.text_span: Accordingly, we converted thiogalactoside 22 into trichlroacetamidate 30 in two steps via hydrolysis of thiogalactoside by using NBS, and subsequent treatment of the so formed hemiacetal with trichloroacetonitrile and DBU.

thought.background: Trichloroacetamidate donor 30 and acceptor 9 are available.
thought.gap: Need to perform glycosylation to form the beta-linked disaccharide 31.
thought.decision: Attempted glycosylation of trichloroacetamidate donor 30 and acceptor 9 with TMSOTf as the promotor at 0 °C, expecting to form beta-linked disaccharide 31.

outcome.observation: Furnished beta-linked disaccharide 31 in 65% yield, along with imidate rearranged side product.

evidence.text_span: Glycosylation of trichloroacetamidate donor 30 and acceptor 9 with TMSOTf as the promotor at 0 °C furnished beta-linked disaccharide 31 in 65% yield, along with imidate rearranged side product.

thought.background: Previous attempt yielded 31 with a side product.
thought.gap: Need to enhance the yield and selectivity of the glycosylation.
thought.decision: To enhance the yield, performed glycosylation reaction at -40 °C using TMSOTf and gradually warmed up to room temperature, expecting exclusive formation of 31.

outcome.observation: The reaction led to exclusive formation of 31 in 87% yield.

evidence.text_span: To enhance the yield of the reaction, we performed glycosylation reaction at -40 °C using TMSOTf and gradually warming up to room temperature. Gratifyingly, the reaction led to exclusive formation of 31 in 87% yield.

thought.background: Disaccharide 31 was successfully synthesized.
thought.gap: Need to cleave the benzoate groups to obtain the diol 32.
thought.decision: To obtain 32, cleaved both benzoate groups using NaOMe in MeOH:CH2Cl2 under reflux, expecting clean deprotection.

outcome.observation: Diol 32 was yielded in excellent yield.

evidence.text_span: After successfully synthesizing disaccharide 31 on gram scale, both the benzoate groups were cleaved using NaOMe in MeOH:CH2Cl2 under reflux to yield diol 32 in excellent yield.

thought.background: Compound 32 is a diol.
thought.gap: Need to regioselectively oxidize the primary hydroxyl group to its corresponding carboxylic acid and then esterify it to obtain the C2'-OH disaccharide acceptor 5.
thought.decision: To obtain 5, regioselectively oxidized the primary hydroxyl group of 32 using TEMPO/BAIB and concomitantly esterified using BnBr and NaHCO3 in DMF, expecting selective oxidation and esterification.

outcome.observation: C2'-OH disaccharide acceptor 5 was furnished as a colourless viscous liquid.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, the primary hydroxyl group of compound 32 was regioselectively oxidized to its corresponding carboxylic acid using TEMPO/BAIB and concomitant esterification employing BnBr and NaHCO3 in DMF furnished C2'-OH disaccharide acceptor 5 in 85% over two steps.

thought.background: Known L-fucose donor 4 and disaccharide acceptor 5 are available.
thought.gap: Need to perform a highly chemoselective glycosylation to form the alpha-linked trisaccharide 33.
thought.decision: To form 33, performed a highly chemoselective glycosylation of L-fucose donor 4 with disaccharide acceptor 5 at -60 °C in the presence of NIS and cat. TMSOTf in CH2Cl2:Et2O (1:1), expecting an alpha-linked trisaccharide as a sole isomer with excellent yield.

outcome.observation: The desired alpha-linked trisaccharide 33 was afforded as a sole isomer with excellent yield. 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and 2D NMR confirmed the newly formed 1,2-cis glycosidic linkage.

evidence.text_span: A highly chemoselective glycosylation reaction of known L-fucose donor 4 with disaccharide acceptor 5 at -60 °C in the presence of NIS (1.2 equiv) and cat. TMSOTf (0.2 equiv) promoter in CH2Cl2:Et2O (1:1) as a participating solvent afforded the desired alpha-linked trisaccharide 33 as a sole isomer with excellent yield. The newly formed 1,2- cis glycosidic linkage was confirmed by 1 H NMR delta 5.38 (d, J = 3.6 Hz, 1H, H-1''), 13C NMR (delta 98.6) and 2D NMR (1H-1H COSY, HSQC)

thought.background: Trisaccharide donor 33 and disaccharide diol acceptor 6 are available.
thought.gap: Need to perform a regioselective (3+2) glycosylation to form the beta-linked pentasaccharide acceptor 34.
thought.decision: To form 34, performed regioselective O3 glycosylation of 3,4-diol acceptor 6 with trisaccharide donor 33 in the presence of NIS and TMSOTf in CH2Cl2 at -60 °C, expecting a clean beta-linked pentasaccharide acceptor with good yield.

outcome.observation: Beta-linked pentasaccharide acceptor 34 was cleanly furnished with good yield. The beta-stereochemistry was confirmed by 1JC-H coupling constant of the anomeric carbon of D-glucosamine unit.

evidence.text_span: Accordingly, regioselective O3 glycosylation of 3,4-diol acceptor 6 with trisaccharide donor 33 in the presence of NIS and TMSOTf promoter in CH2Cl2 at -60 °C cleanly furnished beta-linked pentasaccharide acceptor 34 with good yield. The beta. - stereochemistry of the newly formed glycosidic linkage was confirmed by 1 JC-H coupling constant of the anomeric carbon of D-glucosamine unit (13C{1H} delta 100.1 : (1JC-H = 161.2 Hz) : ppm).

thought.background: Pentasaccharide 34 was obtained.
thought.gap: Need to confirm the regioselectivity of the glycosylation by acetylating the C4'-OH.
thought.decision: To confirm regioselectivity, acetylated C4'-OH in 34 using acetic anhydride, triethylamine, and DMAP to obtain 34a, expecting a downfield shift in 1H NMR for the acetylated proton.

outcome.observation: Compound 34a was yielded as a viscous liquid. The downfield shift of a doublet at delta 5.13 ppm, J = 3.2 Hz in 1H NMR spectrum clearly confirmed the presence of acetate group at C-4' position in 34a.

evidence.text_span: The regioselectivity was confirmed by acetylation of C4'-OH in 34 using acetic anhydride, triethylamine and DMAP to obtain 34a in 87% yield. The downfield shift of a doublet at delta 5.13 ppm, J = 3.2 Hz in 1H NMR spectrum clearly confirmed the presence of acetate group at C-4' position in 34a

thought.background: Pentasaccharide acceptor 34 and D-quinovosamine donor 7 are available.
thought.gap: Stereoselective beta-(1,4) glycosylation of poorly nucleophilic C4-OH of the 1,3-disubstituted galactose moiety is very difficult.
thought.decision: Attempted coupling of thioglycoside donor 7 with poorly nucleophilic 4'-OH pentasaccharide acceptor 34 in the presence of NIS and TMSOTf in CH2Cl2 at -60 °C, expecting to form beta-linked hexasaccharide 3.

outcome.observation: Beta-linked hexasaccharide 3 was afforded in a low yield of 25%, and the rest of the acceptor remained unreacted while the donor was hydrolyzed.

evidence.text_span: First, we tried coupling of thioglycoside donor 7 with poorly nucleophilic 4'-OH pentasaccharide acceptor 34 in the presence of NIS and TMSOTf promoter in CH2Cl2 at -60 °C to afford beta. - linked hexasaccharide 3 in a low yield of 25% and rest of the acceptor remained unreacted while donor was hydrolysed (Table 3, entry 1).

thought.background: Previous attempt with NIS/TMSOTf yielded low product.
thought.gap: Need to find a milder promoter for the glycosylation.
thought.decision: Attempted glycosylation by replacing TMSOTf with a mild promoter AgOTf under identical conditions, expecting improved yield.

outcome.observation: Did not lead to product; acceptor was recovered.

evidence.text_span: Replacing TMSOTf with a mild promoter AgOTf under identical conditions did not lead to product (Table 3, entry 2).

thought.background: Previous attempts with thioglycoside donor 7 failed or yielded low product.
thought.gap: Need to use a more reactive donor for the glycosylation.
thought.decision: Attempted glycosylation using a reactive trichloroacetimidate donor with TMSOTf at -60 °C, expecting moderate improvement in yield.

outcome.observation: Led to moderate improvement in the yield (51%), and acceptor was recovered.

evidence.text_span: The modest yield of the glycosylation reaction can be attributed to steric hinderance to form highly branched (1,3,4-tri-substituent) galactopyranose containing hexasaccharide. Due to this, a significant portion of donor was hydrolysed. So, it was envisioned that increasing the donor concentration would probably increase the yield of reaction. Accordingly, we increased the equivalents of donor and performed the coupling with 2 equivalents of imidate donor with pentasaccharide acceptor 34. To our delight, the yield of the reaction was improved to 64% under this condition and the remaining acceptor was recovered (Table 3, entry 5).

thought.background: Previous attempt with trichloroacetimidate donor yielded 51%.
thought.gap: Need to use a more stable imidate donor for the glycosylation.
thought.decision: Attempted glycosylation using the corresponding stable N-phenyltrifluoroacetimidate donor with TMSOTf at -60 °C, expecting moderate improvement in yield.

outcome.observation: Led to moderate improvement in the yield (50%), and acceptor was recovered.

evidence.text_span: The modest yield of the glycosylation reaction can be attributed to steric hinderance to form highly branched (1,3,4-tri-substituent) galactopyranose containing hexasaccharide. Due to this, a significant portion of donor was hydrolysed. So, it was envisioned that increasing the donor concentration would probably increase the yield of reaction. Accordingly, we increased the equivalents of donor and performed the coupling with 2 equivalents of imidate donor with pentasaccharide acceptor 34. To our delight, the yield of the reaction was improved to 64% under this condition and the remaining acceptor was recovered (Table 3, entry 5).

thought.background: Previous attempts yielded low product due to steric hindrance and donor hydrolysis.
thought.gap: Need to increase the donor concentration to improve the yield of the reaction.
thought.decision: Increased the equivalents of imidate donor to 2 equivalents and performed the coupling with pentasaccharide acceptor 34, expecting improved yield.

outcome.observation: The yield of the reaction was improved to 64%, and the remaining acceptor was recovered.

evidence.text_span: The modest yield of the glycosylation reaction can be attributed to steric hinderance to form highly branched (1,3,4-tri-substituent) galactopyranose containing hexasaccharide. Due to this, a significant portion of donor was hydrolysed. So, it was envisioned that increasing the donor concentration would probably increase the yield of reaction. Accordingly, we increased the equivalents of donor and performed the coupling with 2 equivalents of imidate donor with pentasaccharide acceptor 34. To our delight, the yield of the reaction was improved to 64% under this condition and the remaining acceptor was recovered (Table 3, entry 5).

thought.background: Previous attempts to form hexasaccharide 3 yielded low product, even with increased donor concentration.
thought.gap: Need to further increase the donor concentration to achieve a high yield.
thought.decision: Further increased the equivalent of quinovosamine donor to 4 equivalents and performed coupling with acceptor 34 in the presence of TMSOTf in CH2Cl2 at -60 °C, expecting a significant increase in yield.

outcome.observation: The yield of 3 was greatly increased to 94%. The beta-stereochemistry was confirmed by 1JC-H coupling constant of the anomeric carbon of D-quinovosamine unit.

evidence.text_span: Encouraged by this result, we further increased the equivalent of quinovosamine donor (4 equivalent) and performed coupling with acceptor 34 in the presence of TMSOTf promoter in CH2Cl2 at -60 °C. The reaction showed a steady progress and after an hour ~20% product was visible on TLC along with intense spots of donor and acceptor. After 2h, about 30% of donor was still prevalent in the reaction, along with small amount of acceptor (TLC analysis). Both donor and acceptors were consumed after 3h and spots of product and hemiacetal resulting from hydrolysis of excess donor were seen on TLC. Gratifyingly, this time, we were able to greatly increase the yield of 3 to 94% (Table 3, entry 6). The selectivity is attributed to the well-known neighbouring group participation of the C-2 amide group of NHTCA. The beta. -stereochemistry of the newly formed glycosidic linkage was confirmed by 1 JC-H coupling constant of the anomeric carbon of D-quinovosamine unit (13C{1H} delta 99.6 (1JC-H = 162.3 Hz)) ppm)

thought.background: Optimized conditions for the synthesis of hexasaccharide 3 are available.
thought.gap: Need to perform a one-pot synthesis of hexasaccharide 3 to improve efficiency.
thought.decision: To synthesize 3 in one-pot, performed chemoselective glycosylation of L-fucose donor 4 with disaccharide acceptor 5, followed by addition of disaccharide acceptor 6, and finally addition of D-quinovosamine donor 7a, all in the same pot with NIS/TMSOTf as promoter, expecting efficient assembly.

outcome.observation: Hexasaccharide 3 was afforded in 68% yield over three steps in a one-pot manner (7.5 h total time), after a single column chromatographic purification.

evidence.text_span: Accordingly, a chemoselective glycosylation of a highly reactive per-O-benzylated thiotolyl fucose donor 8 and 2'-OH disaccharide acceptor 9 using NIS/TMSOTf as the promoter employing CH2Cl2:Et2O as a solvent mixture at -60 °C was conducted as before to cleanly afford the corresponding trisaccharide intermediate 33 in 3h. Further, addition of glycosyl acceptor 6 in the same pot in the presence of NIS/TMSOTf promoter led to the formation of the corresponding 4'-OH pentasaccharide acceptor 34. After 2.5 h, donor 7a (4 eq) and TMSOTf (0.8 eq) were added in the same pot at -60 °C to furnish hexasaccharide 3 in 68% yield over three steps in a one-pot manner (7.5 h total time), after a single column chromatographic purification.

thought.background: Hexasaccharide 3 was successfully synthesized.
thought.gap: Need to regioselectively open the benzylidene ring to expose the 4-OH for phosphorylation.
thought.decision: To obtain 36, performed a regioselective O4 reductive benzylidene ring opening of fully functionalized hexasaccharide 3 using Et3SiH and TfOH at -78 °C, expecting to expose the 4-OH nucleophile.

outcome.observation: The desired 4-OH nucleophile 36 was yielded.

evidence.text_span: Using Et3SiH and TfOH at -78 °C, a regioselective O4 reductive benzylidene ring opening of fully functionalized hexasaccharide 3, led to the desired 4-OH nucleophile 36 in 75% yield.

thought.background: Compound 36 has an exposed 4-OH group.
thought.gap: Need to phosphorylate the 4-OH group with phosphonic acid 35.
thought.decision: Attempted phosphorylation of compound 36 with phosphonic acid 35 in the presence of DCC, expecting to form the phosphorylated hexasaccharide.

alternatives_considered.strategy: Using different bases (1H-tetrazole, pyridine, Et3N, DIPEA) along with DCC
alternatives_considered.rejected_because: No phosphorylated compound was obtained, indicating poor nucleophilicity of 4-OH D-glucosamine in the sterically crowded hexasaccharide 36.

outcome.observation: No phosphorylated compound was detected in TLC or in mass spectrum, and the hexasaccharide 36 remained as such. The poor nucleophilicity of 4-OH D-glucosamine in the sterically crowded hexasaccharide 36 was considered to be the main factor behind the failure of the coupling.

evidence.text_span: Next, we attempted phosphorylation of compound 36 with phosphonic acid 35 in the presence of DCC. However, no phosphorylated compound was detected in TLC or in mass spectrum and the hexasaccharide 36 remained as such. Although different types of bases such as 1H-terazole, pyridine, Et3N or DIPEA along with DCC were tried for coupling of 4-OH 36 with phosphonate linker 35, no phosphorylated compound was obtained. The poor nucleophilicity of 4-OH D-glucosamine in the sterically crowded hexasaccharide 36 was considered to be the main factor behind the failure of the coupling.

thought.background: Previous attempts to phosphorylate 36 failed due to poor nucleophilicity of the 4-OH group.
thought.gap: Need a highly electrophilic phosphorus (V) coupling partner for phosphorylation.
thought.decision: To phosphorylate 36, reacted it with freshly prepared bis(chloro)-(2-azidoethyl) phosphonate 37 in the presence of 0.45 M tetrazole in CH3CN and DIPEA, followed by addition of water, expecting rapid esterification and C-P bond formation. Then, performed global deprotection using H2 and Pd(OH)2/C to obtain the target molecule 1.

outcome.observation: The corresponding phosphorylated hexasaccharide was formed, confirmed by 31P NMR and HRMS. Finally, hydrogenolysis of all Bn, NAP groups and conversion of NHTCA to NHAc furnished the phosphorylated hexasaccharide repeating unit 1 in 68% yield over three steps.

evidence.text_span: Gratifyingly, phosphorylation of 4-OH hexasaccharide 3 with freshly prepared bis(chloro)-(2-azidoethyl) phosphonate 37 in the presence of 0.45 M tetrazole in CH3CN and DIPEA, followed by addition of water in the same pot resulted in the formation of the corresponding phosphorylated hexasaccharide which was confirmed from 31P phosphorus NMR and HRMS. Finally, hydrogenolysis of all the Bn, NAP groups and conversion of NHTCA to NHAc was performed using H2, Pd(OH)2/C in EtOH at room temperature followed by purification through Sephadex G25 column to furnish the phosphorylated hexasaccharide repeating unit 1 of PS B from Bacteroides fragilis in 61% yield over three steps.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for the total synthesis.

validation.significance: This work reports the first total synthesis of a structurally complex zwitterionic hexasaccharide repeating unit of polysaccharide B from Bacteroides fragilis. It addresses significant synthetic challenges, including the synthesis of rare deoxy amino sugars, stereoselective installation of 1,2-cis and 1,2-trans glycosidic linkages, glycosylation of sterically hindered acceptors, and late-stage phosphorylation. The convergent one-pot glycosylation strategy is efficient, and the synthesized ZPS RU 1 is poised for immunological studies for vaccine development.

source.title: Total synthesis of a structurally complex zwitterionic hexasaccharide repeating unit of polysaccharide B from Bacteroides fragilis via one-pot glycosylation.

pubchem_fills.name: phosphorylated zwitterionic hexasaccharide repeating unit of PS B from Bacteroides fragilis
